Review safety guide
01 · Foundations

Build confidence before intensity.

For a first course, the goal is not to exhaust the room. It is to create a ride that feels clear, safe, musical, and achievable. Every standing section is optional, and the instructor should coach effort—not force a fixed resistance.

2–3Easy
Full conversation
4Comfortable
Working pace
5–6Moderate
Talk, not sing
7–8Hard
Short phrases
9–10Maximal
Not used today

The essential rule

Cadence and resistance work together. If a rider’s hips bounce or the pedals feel uncontrolled, ask them to slow down or add a small amount of resistance.

Cadence ceiling

Keep sustained work under approximately 105–110 RPM. Beginners should choose the highest cadence they can control with quiet hips and smooth pedal strokes.

Warm-up

Allow 5–10 minutes for heart rate and breathing to rise gradually. Start seated, light, and technically focused.

Cooldown

Reduce resistance and cadence gradually. Avoid an abrupt stop; let breathing settle before riders dismount.

Cue the change before the music changes: position → cadence → resistance → effort.

First-timer safety note

Published case reviews show that severe exertional rhabdomyolysis has occurred disproportionately after first spinning sessions. Avoid maximal work. Stop for chest pain, faintness, severe weakness, or unusual breathlessness. Extreme thigh pain or dark urine after class requires urgent medical attention.

RPE = rating of perceived exertion on a 0–10 scale.
02
02 · Today’s first ride · used by the live console

The rhythm ride.

This is the class used by the live console: three tracks to warm up, alternating cadence and climbing work, one controlled final build, then a calm finish. Standing is always optional.

Local music setup: Keep the five numbered program folders inside one Spinning Audio folder. Connect that parent folder before class and keep a backup device/cable. The browser never uploads or caches the music files.
The live console pairs each local song with its exercise, cover, artist information, and timer.

Music energy map

1 · Welcome

Warm, familiar, low-pressure.

2 · Prepare

Clear beat, light lift in energy.

3 · Work

Heavy, driving tracks for hills.

4 · Release

Brighter, lighter recovery tracks.

5 · Peak

Fast intervals, then one emotional finale.

6 · Land

Calm final track, no abrupt stop.

Do not reuse Today’s Ride playlist: choose songs whose lengths match these alternative blocks.
05
05 · Instructor language

Simple cues that land.

Before acceleration

“In ten seconds, keep your hips quiet and let the legs get a little quicker. Your resistance stays supportive.”

Before a climb

“Add resistance in small steps. The road gets heavier, but your pedal stroke stays smooth.”

Before standing

“Standing is optional. Add resistance first, then rise with your hips over the saddle and your hands light.”

During recovery

“Keep the legs moving. Let your breath settle, relax your jaw, and take water if you need it.”

When form slips

“If your hips are bouncing, add a touch of resistance or slow the legs. Control is the goal.”

During the final effort

“Choose the strongest pace you can control today. This is hard, not reckless—leave room to finish well.”

Pre-class checklist

  • Rehearse the entire playlist with a stopwatch.
  • Confirm clean/explicit versions in the studio account.
  • Test microphone, speaker level, and backup audio.
  • Learn the bike’s brake and resistance controls.
  • Arrive early to help with saddle and handlebar setup.
  • Identify first-time riders and relevant injuries.
  • Explain RPE and that recovery is always allowed.
  • Make every standing section optional.
  • Keep water accessible and the room ventilated.
  • Never cue zero resistance or uncontrolled cadence.
  • Avoid push-ups, crunches, weights, and complex choreography.
  • End with a gradual cooldown before dismounting.

The first-course objective

Riders should leave understanding the bike, trusting the instructor, and feeling successful enough to return—not wondering whether they survived.

Best teaching rhythm: name the change → explain how → offer an option → encourage.
06
06 · Research notes

Sources + responsible use.

View 6 research and safety references
American Heart Association — Warm Up, Cool Downheart.org/…/warm-up-cool-down

Supports gradual 5–10 minute warm-ups and cooldowns rather than stopping suddenly.

Centers for Disease Control and Prevention — Measuring Physical Activity Intensitycdc.gov/physical-activity-basics/measuring

Defines the 0–10 relative intensity scale and talk test used in this guide.

Indoor Cycling Association — Why Cadence Mattersindoorcyclingassociation.com/…/why-cadence-matters

Discusses cadence/resistance interaction, stable form, progressive cadence work, and an approximate 110 RPM ceiling.

Terry et al. (2020) — Effects of music in exercise and sportdoi.org/10.1037/bul0000216

Meta-analysis of 139 studies; music was associated with improved affect, performance, perceived exertion, and efficiency.

Masuda et al. (2023) — Exertional rhabdomyolysis after indoor spinningpubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35254210

Systematic review of 97 reported patients; many were healthy women attending their first spinning session.

Chavarrias et al. (2019) — Health Benefits of Indoor Cyclingpubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31443139

Systematic review of indoor cycling’s health and fitness effects.

Important: This document is an educational planning aid, not medical guidance and not a replacement for a recognized indoor-cycling instructor certification, CPR/AED training, the studio’s emergency procedures, or participant screening. The instructor remains responsible for music licensing, local facility policy, bike setup, room conditions, and adapting the class to the people present.

Next rehearsal

Download the PDF and print pages 3–4. Place them on the instructor console, then run the full 44:56 sequence twice: once for movement timing and once for cue timing and microphone practice.
